headlines.title: STAPLE TRADE OPENS ACTIVE; DEMAND GOOD
headlines.subtitle: Movement to Inland Points Show Liberal Increase Over Previous Week.
headlines.fullText: The jobbing markets opened with a fair volume of business Tuesday. Country merchants are placing larger orders for staples, and the movement to Inland points is considerably increased. Shipments are going forward in a greater volume than for several weeks. Prices on staples are firm, but practically no changes are announced. The cotton crop is expected to begin to move more rapidly, and with the combined efforts of banks and merchants the farmer will be able to realize cash from his cotton as fast as picked and ready to market. This, it is belleved by business men, will soon place business on a formal basis. Green vegetables are offered in liberal quantities. The demand is good and prices unchanged from the close of last week. The market continues to be well supplied with poultry and eggs, and prices here are easter.

headlines.title: Hogs Big Dime Higher; Cattle Remain Steady
headlines.subtitle: Twenty-Two Carloads of Mexican Steers Sell at $5.45-Best Beeves Make $7.50-Choice Hogs Sell at $9.30 to $9.35.
headlines.fullText: More than fifty carloada of M xlean cattle were on hand Tuesday, several carlonds from Oklahoma and Texas shipments were shown, and country points were liberal. To-tal calves. receipta " General market was very slow. 4,000 cattle and 800 but prices never varied far from steady with Monday's basis. Twenty-two car-loads of Mexican steers sold at $5.45. a price that looked 10 centa higher than last week's close. String of 1.048 Mexi-can cows, averaging 634 pounds, sold to a feeder buyer at $4.45. Mexican calves brought $1.50 to $6. Heat fat steers notched $ Quotations: Beeves, $5.25 to $7.50; stockers, $5.25 to $6.76; cows, $3.25 to $5.75; heifers, $5.50 to $7.25: bulls, 4.25 to $5.50; calves, $4.50 to $8. Buyers took care of a supply 1,500 hogs at prices that were gener-ally a big dime higher. One car yellow. brought $9.35 for a top, and bulk of the crop went at $9 to $9.25. Sheep market was active and strong Two decks of choice Mexican wethers sold at $5.75 and four decks of weth ers and ewes made $5.50. Receipts were close to 1,000 head.

headlines.title: WHEAT LOWER; WAR REPORTS HELP TO BEARS
headlines.subtitle: Heavy Selling Follows News of Success of Allied Forces.
headlines.fullText: CHICAGO, Berit. 8 .- Wheat swept downward today, influenced by reports of big victories for the alves near Paris and because of talk that pence might come with as surprising suddenness as had characterized the beginning of the war. General selling set in at the start and there was no check to the decline until prices had receded as much as 6% cents In some cases. The greatest setback was in the December delivery, which fall to $1.16, as com-pared with $1.22% saturday night. La-ter December rallled to $1.19. Changes In the other months were less violent. Corn turned heavy with wheat. Longa showed a noticeable disposition to real-ize. After opening Ne to 1%e ad-vance, the market underwent an all-round decline, averaging 1c. Oats responded to peace talk almost as readily as wheat did. It was said that export sales were email. Grain weakness made Itself felt with decisive effect on provisions. Higher prices for hogs formed only a tem-porary obstacle.

headlines.title: IMPROVEMENT INDICATED IN COTTON CROP
headlines.subtitle: Ginning Figures of Only 475,-455 Bales Show Late-ness of Crop.
headlines.fullText: The report on the number of bales of cotton ginned up to Sept. 1 this year, aggregating 475,455 bales, against 794,-006 last year, 750,935 the year before. 771,297 in 1911 and 353,011 in 1910. an Indication of the lateness of the crop. It is believed that there is a wonder-ful improvement in the condition of cotton since the last government con-dition report was made, and that this year promises a bumper cotton crop. The government weather map shows clear over the entire cotton belt, with higher temperatures, and much Im-provement has taken place. The sta-ple is better and buyers are more ad-tive. Reports of spot sales in the South are more numerous and on a fraction-ally higher basis. Monday and Tues-day a considerable amount of cotton is reported to have been taken In Texas at prices ranging around 6%0 for mid-dling. However, at these figures no cotton is pressed for sale.

headlines.title: FALLS FROM MOTORCYCLE; DEATH LIKELY
headlines.subtitle: Clyde Armstrong Sustains Probably Fatal Injuries; Friend Badly Hurt.
headlines.fullText: Clyde Arrastrong, 23, 2812 Travis avenue, is in the Chy-County nos-pital suffering frot. concussion of the brain and various other injuries which are esuit likely to cause his death as a result of a fall from a motorcycle on Arlington Heights Fied Monday ernoon. John Bozeman, 18, 1301 Johnson street, who was riding a few feet be-hind Armstrong, s in the hospital with a frac-tured collar bone "Red" Helmcamp, In a semicon-scwus conutuun as a result of a mo-torcycle acident on the same road ten days ago. Doctors have about given up hope for Helmenmp's recovery. He Is a lose friend of both Armstrong and Bozeman, and participated In the Fourth of July run with them. Armstrong has never regained con-sclousness. hend In addition to the wound on his head, he is suffering with a shattered collar bone and a broken right ankle. He was already crippled in his left leg. Odds are against his recovery. Bozeman sald e and Armstrong were traveling at thirty-five miles an hour In front of the Zelosky rest-dence. Bozeman said he saw Arm-strong's machine slide out from un-der him. He was only a few feet be-hind, and tried to run around Arm-strong's wrecked machine, but struck a part of it and was thrown head first against the street car track. He landed on his shoulder. An automobile took both boys to the hospital. The machines were not badly damaged.

headlines.title: CITY SCHOOL DISTRICT LINES ARE CHANGED
headlines.subtitle: Tyra's Successor Will Be Named at October Meeting.
headlines.fullText: New street lines. marking publie school by districta, were announced by Superintendent Cantwell as fot-lows: South Side-Line between George C. Clarke and E. M. Daggett School In Lowden street. All pupils south of line below seventh grade attend Clarke School; all other pupils in seventh, bigli and low, attend Dags All other pu-pils south of Myrtle street in high sev-enth grade will attend Daggett. Low seventh grade pupils will attend as usu-al, their respective schools. The line dividing the new seventh district from the old seventh district is Maddox east to New York to Myrtle to Illinois to Magnolia to the I. & G. N. tracks. All pupils above the fifth grade n both divisions will attend the Carro !! . Peak School; below the fifth grade pupils will attend schools In their re-spective districts. North Side-The dividing line be-tween Circle Park and Denver Ave-nte Schools will be Twentieth street. from Lee avenue west. The dividing line between M. G. El-lis, Denver Avenue and Circle Park Schools Is Lee avenue. All North Side pupils from low sev-enth to eleventh grades will go to Cir-cle Park; also grades one to four north of Twentieth street and west of Lee to Market street, go to Circle Park. other North Side schools have grades ne to six in their respective districte. The school board has named Mies Mattie Beth Morgan as instructor In toology in Central High School, Miss Irma Poindexter as departmental teacher of muste in the sixth and eighth districts: J. C. Cain In commercial course, Central High; Miss Myrtle Mc-Peak and Miss Mary Faires, teachers in kindergarten The board at its October meeting will elect a successor to E. T. Tyra, former president, who resigned Monday. It al-so will fill the vacancy created by his resignation. George S. Clough, Instructor in phys Ice, Central High School, resigned to become principal of the Corsicana High School.

headlines.title: REGISTRY OF STUDENTS AT T. C. U. HEAVY
headlines.fullText: Dining halls at Texas Christian Uni-versity were filled to overflowing Tuesday at lunch, as evidence of the tremendous registry of students in the twenty-four hours. With the exception of a few rooms In Price, Hall, the boys' dormitory, the dormitories were fillel to capacity by Tuesday at noon. The girls' dormitory is filled, and if any other giris arrive they will have to be put in rooms with the others. Fifty matriculated Tuesday morning, it was announced. All of the faculty have arrived Among the late arrivals are Prof. and Mrs. W. H. Batson from Osborn, Ohio. When Prof. Batson closed the season at T. C. U. last term and went to Ohto he was a single man. Only ten days ago he married Miss Winifred Creamer. Prof. Batson was superintendent of public schools at Osborn for several years.

headlines.title: SANITARY HOME BURNED
headlines.subtitle: Family Went to Sleep With Lamp Burning-Loss $1,700.
headlines.fullText: The home of Arthur T. Tewmey. 2808 South Jones street, was burned at 2:30 . m. Tuesday, When the family re-tired a lamp was left burning on a ta-ble. That room was in flames when the family awoke. The loss is estimated at $1.700. Insurance was $1,000 on building and 5350 on contents.

headlines.title: WORK PRAISED
headlines.fullText: Strong resolutions Indorsing the work of the state livestock sanitary commission and the bureau of animal industry in Texas and urging upon the governor the reappointment of all of the present members of the commis-sion were adopted by the executive committee of the Cattle Raisers' Asso-ciation of Texas, in session Tuesday morning at the Westbrook hotel. The resolutions further urge members of the association to co-op-erate with the state and Federal offi cials to rid the state of ticks, scabies and other livestock diseases. Favorable action was taken upon 136 new applications for membership These applicants represented a total of 45,642 cattle. The members of the committee in attendance at this meeting were: J. Jackson, Alpine: Sam Davidson, Fort Worth: S. B. Burnett, Fort Worth; F. S. Hastings, Stamford; John McBain, Trinidad, Colo .; N. H. Corder, Sander-Bon; R. J. Cook, Beville; L. Ward, San Antonio: H. C. Harding, Amarillo:, W. E. Weatherabee, Del Rio; M. Sansom, Fort Worth, and J. M. Bassett, Cros-byton.

headlines.title: Judge and Lawyer Will be Guardians of Convicted Girl
headlines.fullText: After Lulu Paxton, 17, had pleaded to forgery and attempting to Sans forged Fred Instrument, in in the Forty-eighth district court Tuesday morning and sentence had been suspended by the jury, Judge R. H. Buck refused to let the case pass pass out of his jurisdiction until he could be assured that the girl would have some place to go when she left the courtroom, which would keep her from drifting Into evil ways. W. D. House, one of the attorneys who had been connected with the case, agreed to take her to his home until place could be found for her. Judge Buck approved that arrangement and said that he would also keep an eye on the girl, as she would be near his own home. The girl's father is dead and she had not been living at home because she could not get along with her stepfa-ther. She admitted trying to pass a check for $28.30 at the L. G. Gilbert store early In August.
